contents.title: Task Response for IELTS Writing Task 2

vocabulary.definition: The assessment criteria in IELTS Writing that evaluates how well a candidate addresses the essay prompt.
examples: A high Task Response score is crucial for a good IELTS band.
Understanding Task Response is key to success in the IELTS Writing test.
vocabulary.context_sentence: In this video, you’ll learn more about Task Response for the IELTS Academic and General Training Writing tests.

vocabulary.definition: The question or statement that you must respond to in an essay.
examples: Carefully analyze the prompt before you start writing.
The essay prompt asked for a discussion of both sides of the argument.
vocabulary.context_sentence: After this prompt, you will need to either answer one or two questions or discuss different viewpoints and express your own opinion.

vocabulary.definition: A particular way of thinking about something; an opinion.
examples: The essay requires you to consider different viewpoints.
The author presents a compelling viewpoint on climate change.
vocabulary.context_sentence: or discuss different viewpoints and express your own opinion.

vocabulary.definition: To deal with or consider a matter or problem.
examples: The essay must address all aspects of the prompt.
The speaker addressed the concerns of the audience.
vocabulary.context_sentence: To achieve Band 6 or higher, you should address all the main parts of the prompt when answering the question.

vocabulary.definition: Connected with or related to something.
examples: Provide relevant examples to support your arguments.
The information presented was not relevant to the topic.
vocabulary.context_sentence: And include any relevant examples from your own knowledge or experience.

vocabulary.definition: A person's opinion on a particular subject.
examples: State your position clearly in the introduction.
The author's position on the issue is well-defined.
vocabulary.context_sentence: In your essay, you should present a position.

vocabulary.definition: Closely connected and appropriate to the topic.
examples: The arguments presented were not directly relevant to the question.
Ensure your arguments are directly relevant to the essay prompt.
vocabulary.context_sentence: For Band 6, your position needs to be directly relevant to the prompt, which means it needs to be relevant to the topic given to you on the question paper.

vocabulary.definition: A conclusion that follows logically from the information presented.
examples: The essay should lead to a logical conclusion.
The evidence presented supported a logical conclusion.
vocabulary.context_sentence: and that your ideas lead to a logical conclusion.

vocabulary.definition: To add more details or information to something.
examples: Extend your ideas with supporting evidence.
The author extended the argument with further examples.
vocabulary.context_sentence: You can ‘extend’ your ideas by going into more detail and you can ‘support’ them by giving examples.

vocabulary.definition: To provide evidence or examples to back up an idea.
examples: Support your arguments with evidence.
The author supported the claims with statistics.
vocabulary.context_sentence: You can ‘extend’ your ideas by going into more detail and you can ‘support’ them by giving examples.

vocabulary.definition: In a way that is good enough or appropriate for a particular purpose or need.
examples: The essay did not adequately address all aspects of the prompt.
The evidence was not adequately presented.
vocabulary.context_sentence: 2. How relevant the main ideas are and how adequately they are extended and supported.

grammar.title: Modal Verb 'should' for Advice/Recommendation
grammar.explanation: Used to give advice or make a recommendation.
examples: You should address all the main parts of the prompt.
Candidates should practice regularly.
grammar.context_sentence: To achieve Band 6 or higher, you should address all the main parts of the prompt when answering the question.

grammar.title: Present Perfect Tense
grammar.explanation: Used to describe an action that started in the past and continues to the present or has a result in the present.
examples: The candidate has discussed the effects.
The essay has come to a logical conclusion.
grammar.context_sentence: In this response, we can see that the candidate has discussed the bad effects these advertisements have on both children, and their families.

grammar.title: Relative Clauses
grammar.explanation: Used to provide additional information about a noun.
examples: advertisements that are aimed at children
the ideas that have been extended
grammar.context_sentence: The point of view that’s put forward is ‘many people believe that these advertisements have a bad effect on children and their families’.

grammar.title: Passive Voice
grammar.explanation: Used when the action is more important than the actor.
examples: you are asked to write an essay
The main ideas are highlighted
grammar.context_sentence: For Task 2, you’re asked to write an essay.

grammar.title: Conditional Sentences (Type 1)
grammar.explanation: Used to express a possible situation and its likely result.
grammar.structure: If + present simple, will + base verb
examples: If you don’t discuss both, you will be limited to Band 5.
If you practice, you will improve.
grammar.context_sentence: If you don’t discuss both, you will be limited to Band 5.

grammar.title: Use of 'both...and'
grammar.explanation: Used to emphasize that two things are included.
examples: both children and their families
both extended and supported
grammar.context_sentence: In this case, you need to discuss whether these advertisements have a bad effect on both children and their families.

contents.title: Step-by-Step Guide to IELTS Writing Task 2

vocabulary.definition: a surprising and previously unknown fact, especially one that is made known in a dramatic way
examples: The discovery of the new planet was a revelation for scientists.
It was a revelation to me how much I enjoyed the book.
vocabulary.context_sentence: I know what a revelation but that's where a lot of marks are lost here is how their official Pand descript is put it the main parts

vocabulary.definition: dealt with or discussed in a suitable way
vocabulary.translation: được giải quyết một cách thích hợp
examples: The issues were appropriately addressed during the meeting.
The report failed to appropriately address the concerns raised.
vocabulary.context_sentence: of the prompt are appropriately addressed let's have a look at the task what parts do you need

vocabulary.definition: the act of moving forward to the next stage in a series
examples: The essay showed a clear progression of ideas.
The company's success was due to its clear progression in the market.
vocabulary.context_sentence: information and ideas are logically organized and there is a clear progression throughout the response if you have a par with the advantages and a paragraph with

vocabulary.definition: a situation in which there are not enough workers available to do the work
examples: The country is facing labor shortages in the healthcare sector.
Labor shortages can negatively impact economic growth.
vocabulary.context_sentence: and it helps societies with labor shortages when there are not enough qualified workers the disadvantages can

vocabulary.definition: disadvantages or problems
examples: One of the drawbacks of living in the city is the high cost of living.
The project had several drawbacks that needed to be addressed.
vocabulary.context_sentence: Among the drawbacks older workers diminishing enthusiasm is often View rily by employers and the second to that we could add their potential

vocabulary.definition: a reduction in the feeling of excitement and interest
examples: The team's diminishing enthusiasm was evident in their poor performance.
The lack of support led to diminishing enthusiasm among the volunteers.
vocabulary.context_sentence: Among the drawbacks older workers diminishing enthusiasm is often View rily by employers and the second to that we could add their potential

vocabulary.definition: an argument or set of reasons put forward to oppose an idea or theory developed in another argument
examples: The lawyer presented a strong counterargument to the prosecution's case.
The author used a counterargument to refute the opposing viewpoint.
vocabulary.context_sentence: this is a counterargument an argument that makes the other point of view weaker now the third idea

vocabulary.definition: to make it difficult for someone to do something or for something to happen
examples: The lack of funding hindered the project's progress.
The weather conditions hindered the rescue operation.
vocabulary.context_sentence: there is also the danger that an order Workforce will hinder the development of young Talent if

vocabulary.definition: enough; adequate
examples: We have sufficient resources to complete the project.
The company did not have sufficient funds to pay its employees.
vocabulary.context_sentence: not allowed sufficient work opportunities and again a counterargument nevertheless any

vocabulary.definition: to state your opinion again, especially in a different way
vocabulary.translation: khẳng định lại ý kiến của bạn
examples: The politician ended the speech by restating his opinion on the issue.
The conclusion should include restating your opinion.
vocabulary.context_sentence: and you should always restate your opinion in your conclusion this is the one key thing you absolutely must do

vocabulary.definition: having enough money to live comfortably
examples: They are financially stable and can afford to buy a house.
Being financially stable reduces stress.
vocabulary.context_sentence: them financially stable or at least much less of a burden on their family or the government

vocabulary.definition: words or phrases used to connect ideas and create a logical flow in writing
examples: Using cohesive devices improves the coherence of an essay.
The essay lacked cohesive devices, making it difficult to follow.
vocabulary.context_sentence: our linkers more formally called cohesive devices that help create this Clarity and logical sequence

grammar.title: Rephrasing the Question
grammar.explanation: Summarizing the task question in your own words in the introduction.
grammar.structure: General statement + Rephrasing the question + Opinion
examples: Many people believe that technology has improved our lives. To what extent do you agree or disagree?
In many countries, the number of elderly people is increasing. Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of this trend.
grammar.context_sentence: first of all we need to summarize the question without simply repeating the same words in many parts

grammar.title: Using 'Some... While others...' to Present Both Sides
grammar.explanation: A useful structure for presenting different viewpoints in the introduction.
grammar.structure: Some + verb + that... While others + verb + that...
examples: Some people believe that online learning is more effective, while others think traditional classrooms are better.
Some argue that governments should invest more in renewable energy, while others believe fossil fuels are still necessary.
grammar.context_sentence: some argue that continuing to work and therefore not being a financial burden on family members or Society is good While others believe it would be better for the elderly to enjoy their

grammar.title: Using 'In my opinion' to State Your Opinion
grammar.explanation: A clear and direct way to express your viewpoint in the introduction.
examples: In my opinion, the benefits of exercise outweigh the risks.
In my opinion, the government should invest more in education.
grammar.context_sentence: now the opinion in my opinion there are more advantages than disadvantages to working

grammar.title: Topic Sentences
grammar.explanation: A sentence at the beginning of a paragraph that introduces the main idea.
grammar.structure: General statement about the paragraph's content.
examples: Firstly, there are several benefits for individuals who work longer.
The disadvantages of this trend are also worth considering.
grammar.context_sentence: it's here to tell us what this whole paragraph is about

grammar.title: Using 'Besides that' to Add Ideas
grammar.explanation: A cohesive device to introduce an additional point.
examples: Besides that, the new policy will create more jobs.
Besides that, the company has increased its profits.
grammar.context_sentence: Besides that to present another idea the knowledge skills and experience they have accumulated

grammar.title: Using 'However' and 'Nevertheless' to Show Contrast
grammar.explanation: Cohesive devices to introduce a contrasting idea or counterargument.
examples: The project faced many challenges. However, the team managed to complete it on time.
The company's sales decreased. Nevertheless, they remained optimistic about the future.
grammar.context_sentence: however it could be argued that sharing different skill sets with younger workers might negate this disadvantage

grammar.title: Complex Sentences
grammar.explanation: Sentences with a variety of structures, including passive voice, modal verbs, and conditionals, to achieve a higher band score.
grammar.structure: Dependent clause + Main clause (e.g., If + clause, + clause)
examples: If the government invests in education, the economy will improve.
Although the weather was bad, we still went for a walk.
grammar.context_sentence: our ability to write complex sentences is important a b s essay will have a variety of complex structures and most sentences will be error free you know sometimes the danger

grammar.title: Passive Voice
grammar.explanation: Used to make the subject of the sentence the receiver of the action.
examples: The report was written by the committee.
The problem is being solved by the team.
grammar.context_sentence: first of all the passive voice enthusiasm is often viewed it could be argued these are complex structures number two modor verbs here

grammar.title: Modal Verbs
grammar.explanation: Used to express possibility, ability, permission, obligation, or advice.
grammar.structure: Modal verb + base form of the verb
examples: We could go to the cinema tonight.
You should study harder for the exam.
grammar.context_sentence: we express possibilities through could and might and make recommendations with should

grammar.title: Conditionals
grammar.explanation: Used to express a condition and its result.
grammar.structure: If + clause, + clause (e.g., If + present simple, + will/future simple)
examples: If it rains, we will stay inside.
If I had more time, I would travel more.
grammar.context_sentence: number three conditionals an older Workforce will hinder the development of young Talent if they're
